food_subgroup: Cooked leafy green vegetable dish with tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Sarson da Saag as a prepared dish; closest match is mustard greens food product (FOODON_03311737). Prepared-dish specificity is not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked mustard leaves as a category; the full Sarson da Saag preparation including ghee tempering and mixed greens is not individually coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Sarson da Saag or mustard greens curry as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sarson da Saag is approximately 200 g (one standard bowl or katori), often accompanied by makki di roti (maize flatbread) and a knob of butter or additional ghee.

nutrient_source.title: Sarson ka saag (Mustard leaves cooked dish) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for cooked mustard leaves (sarson ka saag) as a prepared dish. Values for the restaurant version are adapted from this reference, with a modest upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for the ghee-based tempering typical of restaurant preparations. Core macros are anchored to the ICMR-NIN cooked-dish entry; secondary nutrients are drawn from the same source where available, with -1 used where the source does not report the nutrient.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cooked leafy green vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked mustard greens and spinach mixed dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Sarson da Saag exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is estimated by analogy with cooked mixed leafy greens (spinach, mustard greens) which are consistently reported in the low-GI range (GI 15–40) in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. The addition of ghee and maize flour further moderates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (32 × 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving) / 100 = 4.2 g. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt and tempering
hypertension.guidance: Sarson da Saag contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one small bowl (150 g) to keep sodium intake within the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from mustard greens
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Sarson da Saag produces a low glycemic load (GL ≈ 4.2 per 200 g serving) and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as large portions of makki di roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High micronutrient density and moderate saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sarson da Saag provides beneficial phytonutrients, folate, iron, and vitamin A from mustard greens; however, the ghee-based tempering contributes approximately 2.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or request preparation with minimal ghee to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Punjabi Sarson Da Saag is a traditional North Indian vegetable dish originating from the Punjab region of India and Pakistan. It is prepared by slow-cooking mustard greens (sarson) together with spinach (palak) and sometimes bathua (chenopodium album) until very soft, then blending or mashing the greens into a thick, velvety purée. The dish is finished with a fragrant tempering (tadka) of onion, garlic, ginger, and green chillies cooked in ghee, which is stirred through the greens to impart richness and depth. A small amount of maize flour (makki atta) is traditionally added to thicken the saag and give it body. The flavour profile is earthy, slightly bitter from the mustard greens, aromatic from the garlic and ginger, and rich from the ghee. The texture is thick, smooth, and hearty. Sarson da Saag is the quintessential winter dish of Punjab and is almost always served with makki di roti (unleavened maize flatbread) and a knob of white butter (makkhan). Nutritionally, the dish is a rich source of dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g), vitamin A (210 mcg RAE/100 g), calcium (155 mg/100 g), iron (3.1 mg/100 g), and folate (48 mcg DFE/100 g), making it highly nutritious. Its low glycemic index (approximately 32) and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (approximately 310 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, Sarson da Saag is served at North Indian restaurants as a seasonal or year-round specialty, typically priced between SGD 13.90 and SGD 15.90 per portion.

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN provides a close match for cooked sarson ka saag as a prepared dish; values are adapted upward for restaurant-style ghee tempering. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked mixed leafy greens as the reference food. No exact restaurant-specific nutrient analysis is available, hence medium confidence.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for cooked mustard leaves (sarson ka saag), with a modest upward adjustment to fat_g and energy_kcal to reflect the ghee-based restaurant tempering. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as ghee from Indian dairy sources contains negligible industrial trans fats (confirmed trace per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal protein; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods, but -1 is used here as the source does not report it explicitly. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mixed leafy greens from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sarson da Saag is available. FoodOn match is closest_parent (mustard greens food product); no exact prepared-dish term exists. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (mustard leaves, cooked) is the closest_parent defensible match. SNOMED CT omitted due to absence of a defensible code. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to ghee. Halal status is assumed as the dish contains no pork or alcohol. Gluten-free status is noted; maize flour is naturally gluten-free.
